main_paper.raw: {"methods":["LLM-QFA (Quantization-Aware Fine-tuning one LLM for All scenarios) framework","Interference-less fine-tuning by decoupling weights of different quantization configurations","Incorporation of Low-Rank adapters (LoRA) for efficient training","Resource-balanced sampling strategy using a non-parametric scheduler to adjust sampling rates for quantization configurations"],"problems":["High memory and computational demands of Large Language Models (LLMs)","Performance degradation due to quantization loss in LLMs","Inefficiency of repeated quantization-aware training (QAT) for multiple deployment scenarios","Interference from weight sharing in once-for-all (OFA) supernets for LLMs","Imbalanced training resource allocation from uniform sampling in OFA methods"],"datasets":["MMLU","Common Sense QA"],"metrics":["Accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"Accuracy (MMLU, INT2 quantization)","value":"~66.0%"},{"metric":"Accuracy (MMLU, INT3 quantization)","value":"~70.0%"},{"metric":"Accuracy (MMLU, INT4 quantization)","value":"~72.0%"}],"novelty_claims":["First introduction of once-for-all (OFA) quantization-aware training for large language models (LLMs)","Decoupling of weights for different quantization configurations to mitigate interference in OFA supernets","Use of Low-Rank adapters (LoRA) to enable efficient fine-tuning of quantized weights","Proposal of a resource-balanced sampling strategy to address imbalance from uniform sampling in OFA training","Demonstration that the framework can efficiently generate diverse optimal quantized models for various deployment scenarios with reduced training cost"]}

methods: LLM-QFA (Quantization-Aware Fine-tuning one LLM for All scenarios) framework
Interference-less fine-tuning by decoupling weights of different quantization configurations
Incorporation of Low-Rank adapters (LoRA) for efficient training
Resource-balanced sampling strategy using a non-parametric scheduler to adjust sampling rates for quantization configurations
problems: High memory and computational demands of Large Language Models (LLMs)
Performance degradation due to quantization loss in LLMs
Inefficiency of repeated quantization-aware training (QAT) for multiple deployment scenarios
Interference from weight sharing in once-for-all (OFA) supernets for LLMs
Imbalanced training resource allocation from uniform sampling in OFA methods
novelty_claims: First introduction of once-for-all (OFA) quantization-aware training for large language models (LLMs)
Decoupling of weights for different quantization configurations to mitigate interference in OFA supernets
Use of Low-Rank adapters (LoRA) to enable efficient fine-tuning of quantized weights
Proposal of a resource-balanced sampling strategy to address imbalance from uniform sampling in OFA training
Demonstration that the framework can efficiently generate diverse optimal quantized models for various deployment scenarios with reduced training cost

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Low-Rank Quantization-Aware Training (LR-QAT)","Low-rank auxiliary weights aligned with quantization grid","Downcasting operator using fixed-point or double-packed integers","Gradient checkpointing for memory efficiency"],"problems":["High computational and memory demands of large language models (LLMs) during quantization-aware training (QAT)","Inefficiency and impracticality of traditional QAT for LLMs due to excessive memory usage","Performance degradation of post-training quantization (PTQ) methods, especially at low-bit quantization (≤4 bits)"],"datasets":["LLaMA-1","LLaMA-2","LLaMA-3","Mistral model families","General language modeling datasets","Common reasoning downstream tasks (zero-shot evaluation)"],"metrics":["Predictive performance (accuracy)","Memory usage (GPU memory required for training)"],"results":[{"metric":"GPU memory usage for QAT on LLaMA-2 7B","value":"Reduced from >98GB to <21GB"},{"metric":"Predictive performance (accuracy)","value":"Matches full-model QAT; outperforms PTQ approaches"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes LR-QAT, a lightweight and memory-efficient QAT algorithm for LLMs that is inference-efficient and does not add overhead at inference time.","Introduces low-rank auxiliary weights that are quantization grid-aware, enabling seamless fusion into quantized weights for efficient inference.","Employs a downcasting operator and gradient checkpointing to drastically reduce memory usage during QAT without sacrificing model performance.","Positions LR-QAT as a general extended pretraining framework, not just a fine-tuning method, allowing the resulting low-bit model to be used for any downstream task.","Demonstrates the ability to train a 7B LLM on a single consumer-grade GPU with 24GB memory, which was previously impractical with traditional QAT."]}

methods: Low-Rank Quantization-Aware Training (LR-QAT)
Low-rank auxiliary weights aligned with quantization grid
Downcasting operator using fixed-point or double-packed integers
Gradient checkpointing for memory efficiency
problems: High computational and memory demands of large language models (LLMs) during quantization-aware training (QAT)
Inefficiency and impracticality of traditional QAT for LLMs due to excessive memory usage
Performance degradation of post-training quantization (PTQ) methods, especially at low-bit quantization (≤4 bits)
metrics: Memory usage (GPU memory required for training)

results.metric: GPU memory usage for QAT on LLaMA-2 7B

results.value: Matches full-model QAT; outperforms PTQ approaches

novelty_claims: Proposes LR-QAT, a lightweight and memory-efficient QAT algorithm for LLMs that is inference-efficient and does not add overhead at inference time.
Introduces low-rank auxiliary weights that are quantization grid-aware, enabling seamless fusion into quantized weights for efficient inference.
Employs a downcasting operator and gradient checkpointing to drastically reduce memory usage during QAT without sacrificing model performance.
Positions LR-QAT as a general extended pretraining framework, not just a fine-tuning method, allowing the resulting low-bit model to be used for any downstream task.
Demonstrates the ability to train a 7B LLM on a single consumer-grade GPU with 24GB memory, which was previously impractical with traditional QAT.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Quantization-Aware Low-Rank Adaptation (QA-LoRA)","Group-wise quantization and adaptation operators"],"problems":["Reducing computational and memory burden of large language models (LLMs) for deployment on edge devices","Maintaining accuracy of LLMs under low-bit quantization","Integrating parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) with quantization without loss of accuracy"],"datasets":["Alpaca","MMLU"],"metrics":["5-shot accuracy on MMLU"],"results":[{"metric":"5-shot MMLU accuracy (QA-LoRA vs QLoRA with PTQ, INT4)","value":"QA-LoRA consistently outperforms QLoRA with PTQ across LLaMA model scales, especially at lower bit widths"},{"metric":"5-shot MMLU accuracy (QA-LoRA vs QLoRA without PTQ)","value":"QA-LoRA is on par with QLoRA without PTQ"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes QA-LoRA, a simple and effective method for quantization-aware low-rank adaptation of LLMs","Introduces group-wise operators to balance the degrees of freedom between quantization and adaptation, improving quantization accuracy and integration of auxiliary weights","QA-LoRA enables efficient fine-tuning with quantized weights and produces a lightweight, quantized model without the need for post-training quantization (PTQ)","QA-LoRA achieves better or comparable accuracy to existing methods while being more efficient in inference and deployment"]}

methods: Quantization-Aware Low-Rank Adaptation (QA-LoRA)
Group-wise quantization and adaptation operators
problems: Reducing computational and memory burden of large language models (LLMs) for deployment on edge devices
Maintaining accuracy of LLMs under low-bit quantization
Integrating parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) with quantization without loss of accuracy

results.metric: 5-shot MMLU accuracy (QA-LoRA vs QLoRA with PTQ, INT4)
results.value: QA-LoRA consistently outperforms QLoRA with PTQ across LLaMA model scales, especially at lower bit widths

results.metric: 5-shot MMLU accuracy (QA-LoRA vs QLoRA without PTQ)
results.value: QA-LoRA is on par with QLoRA without PTQ

novelty_claims: Proposes QA-LoRA, a simple and effective method for quantization-aware low-rank adaptation of LLMs
Introduces group-wise operators to balance the degrees of freedom between quantization and adaptation, improving quantization accuracy and integration of auxiliary weights
QA-LoRA enables efficient fine-tuning with quantized weights and produces a lightweight, quantized model without the need for post-training quantization (PTQ)
QA-LoRA achieves better or comparable accuracy to existing methods while being more efficient in inference and deployment

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["SmoothQuant: a training-free, accuracy-preserving, general-purpose post-training quantization (PTQ) method for LLMs that migrates quantization difficulty from activations to weights via a mathematically equivalent per-channel scaling transformation","Implementation of three efficiency levels of quantization settings (O1-O3) within SmoothQuant","Integration of SmoothQuant into FasterTransformer for efficient serving"],"problems":["High compute and memory requirements of large language models (LLMs) during inference","Existing quantization methods for LLMs cannot maintain both accuracy and hardware efficiency, especially for activations with outliers","Difficulty in quantizing activations in LLMs due to systematic outliers, leading to accuracy degradation","Lack of a training-free, hardware-friendly, and efficient quantization scheme for LLMs that uses INT8 for all compute-intensive operations"],"datasets":["OPT-175B","BLOOM-176B","GLM-130B","MT-NLG 530B","Llama-1/2","Falcon","Mistral","Mixtral"],"metrics":["Accuracy (task-specific, e.g., perplexity or downstream task accuracy)","Inference speedup (e.g., speedup factor over FP16)","Memory reduction (e.g., memory usage compared to FP16)"],"results":[{"metric":"Inference speedup (PyTorch)","value":"up to 1.51x"},{"metric":"Memory saving (PyTorch)","value":"up to 1.96x"},{"metric":"Inference speedup (FasterTransformer)","value":"up to 1.56x"},{"metric":"Memory usage (FasterTransformer)","value":"halved compared to FP16"},{"metric":"Accuracy loss","value":"negligible"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a mathematically equivalent per-channel scaling transformation to migrate quantization difficulty from activations to weights, smoothing activation outliers and enabling effective INT8 quantization for both weights and activations in LLMs","SmoothQuant is training-free, general-purpose, and compatible with various quantization schemes","SmoothQuant enables efficient, hardware-friendly INT8 quantization for all matrix multiplications in LLMs, including very large models (up to 530B parameters)","SmoothQuant allows serving extremely large LLMs (e.g., 530B) within a single node, reducing hardware costs and democratizing LLM deployment"]}

methods: SmoothQuant: a training-free, accuracy-preserving, general-purpose post-training quantization (PTQ) method for LLMs that migrates quantization difficulty from activations to weights via a mathematically equivalent per-channel scaling transformation
Implementation of three efficiency levels of quantization settings (O1-O3) within SmoothQuant
Integration of SmoothQuant into FasterTransformer for efficient serving
problems: High compute and memory requirements of large language models (LLMs) during inference
Existing quantization methods for LLMs cannot maintain both accuracy and hardware efficiency, especially for activations with outliers
Difficulty in quantizing activations in LLMs due to systematic outliers, leading to accuracy degradation
Lack of a training-free, hardware-friendly, and efficient quantization scheme for LLMs that uses INT8 for all compute-intensive operations
metrics: Accuracy (task-specific, e.g., perplexity or downstream task accuracy)
Inference speedup (e.g., speedup factor over FP16)
Memory reduction (e.g., memory usage compared to FP16)
novelty_claims: Proposes a mathematically equivalent per-channel scaling transformation to migrate quantization difficulty from activations to weights, smoothing activation outliers and enabling effective INT8 quantization for both weights and activations in LLMs
SmoothQuant is training-free, general-purpose, and compatible with various quantization schemes
SmoothQuant enables efficient, hardware-friendly INT8 quantization for all matrix multiplications in LLMs, including very large models (up to 530B parameters)
SmoothQuant allows serving extremely large LLMs (e.g., 530B) within a single node, reducing hardware costs and democratizing LLM deployment

methods: QLoRA: Efficient finetuning of quantized LLMs by backpropagating through a frozen 4-bit quantized pretrained model into Low Rank Adapters (LoRA)
4-bit NormalFloat (NF4): A new quantization data type optimized for normally distributed weights
Double Quantization: Quantizing the quantization constants to further reduce memory footprint
Paged Optimizers: Using NVIDIA unified memory to manage memory spikes during training
Tournament-style benchmarking with both human and GPT-4 evaluation for chatbot performance
problems: High memory requirements for finetuning large language models (LLMs), making it infeasible on standard hardware
Existing quantization methods only work for inference, not for training/finetuning
Need for efficient, accessible finetuning of very large models (e.g., 65B parameters) without performance degradation
Lack of trustworthy and cost-effective evaluation methods for chatbot performance
datasets: 8 instruction following datasets (not all named explicitly)
novelty_claims: First demonstration of finetuning a quantized 4-bit model without performance degradation compared to 16-bit finetuning
Introduction of 4-bit NormalFloat (NF4), an information-theoretically optimal quantization data type for normally distributed weights
Introduction of Double Quantization to further reduce memory usage by quantizing quantization constants
Introduction of Paged Optimizers to handle memory spikes during training
Ability to finetune a 65B parameter model on a single 48GB GPU, making large model finetuning accessible
Release of a large collection of open-source adapters and CUDA kernels for 4-bit training
Comprehensive analysis showing that data quality is more important than dataset size for instruction following tasks
Demonstration that GPT-4-based evaluation is a cheap and reasonable alternative to human evaluation for chatbot benchmarking

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["LoftQ (LoRA-Fine-Tuning-aware Quantization): a quantization framework that jointly quantizes a large language model and finds a proper low-rank initialization for LoRA fine-tuning, integrating low-rank approximation with quantization to better align with original pre-trained weights."],"problems":["Performance gap between full fine-tuning and quantization plus LoRA fine-tuning in large language models, especially at low-bit quantization (e.g., 2-bit, 4-bit).","Degradation of LoRA fine-tuning performance due to poor initialization when applied to quantized models, particularly in low-bit regimes.","Ineffective adaptation of quantized pre-trained models to downstream tasks due to quantization-induced discrepancies."],"datasets":["XSum (Narayan et al., 2018)","CNN/DailyMail (Hermann et al., 2015)","MNLI (Wang et al., 2019)","SQuADv1.1 (Rajpurkar et al., 2016)","WikiText-2"],"metrics":["Rouge-1","Accuracy (for MNLI)","F1 score (for SQuADv1.1)","Perplexity (for language modeling on WikiText-2)","Spectral norm and Frobenius norm of initialization discrepancy"],"results":[{"metric":"Rouge-1 (XSum, 4-bit quantization)","value":"+1.1 over QLoRA"},{"metric":"Rouge-1 (CNN/DailyMail, 4-bit quantization)","value":"+0.8 over QLoRA"},{"metric":"Accuracy (MNLI, 2-bit quantization)","value":"over 8% gain over QLoRA"},{"metric":"F1 (SQuADv1.1, 2-bit quantization)","value":"more than 10% gain over QLoRA"},{"metric":"Perplexity (WikiText-2, LLAMA-2-13b, 2-bit and 4-bit)","value":"LoftQ achieves lower perplexity than initialization.","Demonstrates that proper low-rank initialization during quantization can significantly reduce the performance gap between quantized+LoRA and full fine-tuning, especially in low-bit regimes.","Shows that LoftQ consistently outperforms existing quantization methods (e.g., QLoRA) across a range of tasks and quantization levels, particularly excelling in challenging 2-bit and methods: LoftQ (LoRA-Fine-Tuning-aware Quantization): a quantization framework that jointly quantizes a large language model and finds a proper low-rank initialization for LoRA fine-tuning, integrating low-rank approximation with quantization to better align with original pre-trained weights.
problems: Performance gap between full fine-tuning and quantization plus LoRA fine-tuning in large language models, especially at low-bit quantization (e.g., 2-bit, 4-bit).
Degradation of LoRA fine-tuning performance due to poor initialization when applied to quantized models, particularly in low-bit regimes.
Ineffective adaptation of quantized pre-trained models to downstream tasks due to quantization-induced discrepancies.
metrics: Spectral norm and Frobenius norm of initialization discrepancy

results.value: LoftQ achieves lower perplexity than QLoRA

results.metric: Spectral/Frobenius norm of initialization discrepancy (BART-large, 2-bit and 4-bit)
results.value: LoftQ significantly reduces discrepancy compared to QLoRA, especially at 2-bit

novelty_claims: Proposes the first quantization framework (LoftQ) that is explicitly aware of and optimized for LoRA fine-tuning, jointly optimizing quantization and low-rank initialization.
Demonstrates that proper low-rank initialization during quantization can significantly reduce the performance gap between quantized+LoRA and full fine-tuning, especially in low-bit regimes.

Shows that LoftQ consistently outperforms existing quantization methods (e.g., QLoRA) across a range of tasks and quantization levels, particularly excelling in challenging 2-bit and mixed-precision settings.
Provides a general framework that works with different quantization methods (e.g., NormalFloat, uniform quantization).

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["EasyQuant: a training-free, data-independent weight-only quantization algorithm for LLMs","Outlier isolation: keeping less than 1% of weight outliers in full precision","Gradient-based optimization of quantization range to minimize reconstruction error","Parallel implementation of quantization for efficiency","Efficient CUDA kernels for outlier isolation in dequantization"],"problems":["High computational and memory cost of large language models (LLMs) hindering deployment","Existing quantization methods require data/calibration sets, risking overfitting and poor generalization","Previous quantization methods are slow and require retraining, which is infeasible for very large models","Need for efficient, data-free quantization methods that guarantee generalization and low overhead"],"datasets":["OPT-176B","BLOOM-176B","LLAMA-65B"],"metrics":["Model accuracy/performance on various benchmarks","Quantization time (efficiency)","System overhead (latency increase)"],"results":[{"metric":"Performance loss after quantization","value":"Almost lossless (comparable to original model)"},{"metric":"System overhead (latency increase) due to outlier handling","value":"<0.1%"},{"metric":"Quantization time for 175B model to 4-bits","value":"10 minutes"},{"metric":"Speed compared to data-dependent methods","value":">10x faster"}],"novelty_claims":["First data-free (data-independent) quantization algorithm for LLMs with almost lossless performance","Does not require any training or calibration data, guaranteeing generalization","Efficient parallel implementation allows quantizing 100B+ parameter models in minutes","Reveals the critical role of outliers in weights for quantization performance and proposes to keep them unquantized","Proposes gradient-based optimization of quantization range for better reconstruction error minimization","Efficient CUDA kernels for outlier isolation with negligible system overhead"]}

methods: EasyQuant: a training-free, data-independent weight-only quantization algorithm for LLMs
Outlier isolation: keeping less than 1% of weight outliers in full precision
Gradient-based optimization of quantization range to minimize reconstruction error
Parallel implementation of quantization for efficiency
Efficient CUDA kernels for outlier isolation in dequantization
problems: High computational and memory cost of large language models (LLMs) hindering deployment
Existing quantization methods require data/calibration sets, risking overfitting and poor generalization
Previous quantization methods are slow and require retraining, which is infeasible for very large models
Need for efficient, data-free quantization methods that guarantee generalization and low overhead

results.metric: System overhead (latency increase) due to outlier handling

novelty_claims: First data-free (data-independent) quantization algorithm for LLMs with almost lossless performance
Does not require any training or calibration data, guaranteeing generalization
Efficient parallel implementation allows quantizing 100B+ parameter models in minutes
Reveals the critical role of outliers in weights for quantization performance and proposes to keep them unquantized
Proposes gradient-based optimization of quantization range for better reconstruction error minimization
Efficient CUDA kernels for outlier isolation with negligible system overhead

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["LLM-QAT: Quantization Aware Training for LLMs using data-free knowledge distillation","Data-free distillation using generated data from the pre-trained model for QAT","Simultaneous quantization of weights, activations, and KV cache"],"problems":["Degradation of LLM performance at low-bit quantization (below 8-bits) using post-training quantization methods","Lack of practical quantization aware training (QAT) methods for LLMs due to data and computational constraints","High memory and throughput bottlenecks in LLMs due to unquantized KV cache and activations"],"datasets":["Generated data from the pre-trained LLaMA models (data-free, not original training data)"],"metrics":["Accuracy (on language modeling tasks)","Model size (memory footprint)","Throughput (inference speed)"],"results":[{"metric":"Quantization level","value":"4-bits for weights and KV cache, 6-bits for activations"},{"metric":"Model accuracy (relative to FP16)","value":"Significant improvements over post-training quantization at 4-bits; larger QAT models outperform smaller FP16 models of similar size"},{"metric":"Data required for QAT","value":"100k generated samples sufficient for effective distillation"}],"novelty_claims":["First application of quantization aware training (QAT) to large language models (LLMs)","Introduction of a data-free knowledge distillation method for QAT, using generated data from the model itself","First demonstration of accurate 4-bit quantized LLMs, including simultaneous quantization of weights, activations, and KV cache","Demonstration that QAT with data-free distillation can outperform post-training quantization and even smaller FP16 models"]}

methods: LLM-QAT: Quantization Aware Training for LLMs using data-free knowledge distillation
Data-free distillation using generated data from the pre-trained model for QAT
Simultaneous quantization of weights, activations, and KV cache
problems: Degradation of LLM performance at low-bit quantization (below 8-bits) using post-training quantization methods
Lack of practical quantization aware training (QAT) methods for LLMs due to data and computational constraints
High memory and throughput bottlenecks in LLMs due to unquantized KV cache and activations
datasets: Generated data from the pre-trained LLaMA models (data-free, not original training data)

results.value: 4-bits for weights and KV cache, 6-bits for activations
Significant improvements over post-training quantization at 4-bits; larger QAT models outperform smaller FP16 models of similar size
100k generated samples sufficient for effective distillation

novelty_claims: First application of quantization aware training (QAT) to large language models (LLMs)
Introduction of a data-free knowledge distillation method for QAT, using generated data from the model itself
First demonstration of accurate 4-bit quantized LLMs, including simultaneous quantization of weights, activations, and KV cache
Demonstration that QAT with data-free distillation can outperform post-training quantization and even smaller FP16 models

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["GPTQ: a one-shot post-training weight quantization method based on approximate second-order information","Bespoke GPU kernels for efficient memory loading and inference with quantized models"],"problems":["High computational and storage costs of large generative pre-trained transformer (GPT/OPT) models, especially for inference","Lack of effective post-training quantization methods that can compress very large language models (hundreds of billions of parameters) to low bitwidths (3-4 bits) without significant accuracy loss","Existing quantization methods either require retraining (which is infeasible for very large models) or do not scale to high compression rates"],"datasets":["OPT-175B (Open Pre-trained Transformer, 175 billion parameters)","BLOOM-176B (BigScience Large Open-science Open-access Multilingual Language Model, 176 billion parameters)"],"metrics":["Perplexity","Inference speedup (relative to FP16)"],"results":[{"metric":"Perplexity increase (OPT-175B, 3-4 bit quantization)","value":"negligible compared to FP16 baseline"},{"metric":"Inference speedup on NVIDIA A100 GPU","value":"~3.25x over FP16"},{"metric":"Inference speedup on NVIDIA A6000 GPU","value":"~4.5x over FP16"},{"metric":"Quantization time for 175B parameter model","value":"~4 GPU hours"}],"novelty_claims":["First post-training quantization method to accurately compress language models with hundreds of billions of parameters to 3-4 bits per weight with negligible accuracy loss","More than doubles the compression gains of previous one-shot quantization methods while preserving accuracy","Enables, for the first time, execution of a 175B parameter model on a single GPU for generative inference","Demonstrates robust results even in extreme quantization regimes (2-bit or ternary quantization)","Provides significant end-to-end inference speedups over FP16 on both high-end and cost-effective GPUs"]}

methods: GPTQ: a one-shot post-training weight quantization method based on approximate second-order information
Bespoke GPU kernels for efficient memory loading and inference with quantized models
problems: High computational and storage costs of large generative pre-trained transformer (GPT/OPT) models, especially for inference
Lack of effective post-training quantization methods that can compress very large language models (hundreds of billions of parameters) to low bitwidths (3-4 bits) without significant accuracy loss
Existing quantization methods either require retraining (which is infeasible for very large models) or do not scale to high compression rates
datasets: BLOOM-176B (BigScience Large Open-science Open-access Multilingual Language Model, 176 billion parameters)

results.metric: Inference speedup on NVIDIA A100 GPU
Inference speedup on NVIDIA A6000 GPU

novelty_claims: First post-training quantization method to accurately compress language models with hundreds of billions of parameters to 3-4 bits per weight with negligible accuracy loss
More than doubles the compression gains of previous one-shot quantization methods while preserving accuracy
Enables, for the first time, execution of a 175B parameter model on a single GPU for generative inference
Demonstrates robust results even in extreme quantization regimes (2-bit or ternary quantization)
Provides significant end-to-end inference speedups over FP16 on both high-end and cost-effective GPUs

results.metric: Average score improvement over base models
Number of LoRA models served on a single NVIDIA A100 GPU (80GB)

novelty_claims: First large-scale, standardized evaluation of 310 LoRA-fine-tuned LLMs across 10 base models and 31 tasks with consistent training and prompting protocols.
Demonstration that 4-bit LoRA fine-tuned models can outperform both their base models and GPT-4 on average across tasks.
Introduction and open-source release of LoRAX, a Multi-LoRA inference server enabling efficient deployment of multiple LoRA models on a single GPU.
Public release of 25 fine-tuned models and their training recipes for community use and replication.
Empirical evidence supporting the cost-effectiveness and quality of deploying multiple specialized LoRA LLMs over a single general-purpose LLM.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Any-precision quantization of LLMs using a lightweight post-training quantization (PTQ) framework that incrementally upscales from low-bit to higher bit-widths while preserving any-precision property","Development of a specialized software engine for efficient serving of any-precision LLMs, including a new GPU kernel and a bit-interleaved (bitplane) memory layout for quantized weights"],"problems":["High deployment costs of multiple, different-sized LLMs due to large memory and compute requirements","Lack of practical methods for any-precision quantization of LLMs without retraining from scratch","Inefficiency of existing GPU kernels for quantized matrix-vector multiplication in supporting memory and bandwidth savings for lower bit-width models"],"datasets":[],"metrics":["Model quality (accuracy/performance at each bit-width)","Inference throughput","Memory footprint"],"results":[{"metric":"Memory footprint","value":"Multiple LLMs quantized to varying bit-widths (e.g., 3, 4, ..., n bits) can be packed into a memory footprint comparable to a single n-bit LLM"},{"metric":"Model quality","value":"Quantized LLMs at each bit-width match the quality of state-of-the-art quantization techniques"},{"metric":"Inference throughput","value":"High inference throughput, matching or outperforming state-of-the-art quantized matrix-vector multiplication engines that do not support any-precision"}],"novelty_claims":["First lightweight, post-training any-precision quantization method for LLMs that does not require retraining from scratch","Specialized software engine and GPU kernel enabling efficient serving and memory bandwidth savings for any-precision LLMs","Ability to overlay multiple, different-sized LLMs into a single memory footprint, enabling low-cost deployment of multiple LLMs with varying bit-widths","Demonstrates state-of-the-art model quality and inference throughput for all supported bit-widths"]}

methods: Any-precision quantization of LLMs using a lightweight post-training quantization (PTQ) framework that incrementally upscales from low-bit to higher bit-widths while preserving any-precision property
Development of a specialized software engine for efficient serving of any-precision LLMs, including a new GPU kernel and a bit-interleaved (bitplane) memory layout for quantized weights
problems: High deployment costs of multiple, different-sized LLMs due to large memory and compute requirements
Lack of practical methods for any-precision quantization of LLMs without retraining from scratch
Inefficiency of existing GPU kernels for quantized matrix-vector multiplication in supporting memory and bandwidth savings for lower bit-width models

results.value: Multiple LLMs quantized to varying bit-widths (e.g., 3, 4, ..., n bits) can be packed into a memory footprint comparable to a single n-bit LLM
Quantized LLMs at each bit-width match the quality of state-of-the-art quantization techniques
High inference throughput, matching or outperforming state-of-the-art quantized matrix-vector multiplication engines that do not support any-precision

novelty_claims: First lightweight, post-training any-precision quantization method for LLMs that does not require retraining from scratch
Specialized software engine and GPU kernel enabling efficient serving and memory bandwidth savings for any-precision LLMs
Ability to overlay multiple, different-sized LLMs into a single memory footprint, enabling low-cost deployment of multiple LLMs with varying bit-widths
Demonstrates state-of-the-art model quality and inference throughput for all supported bit-widths

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["SqueezeLLM post-training quantization framework","Sensitivity-based non-uniform quantization (allocating quantization bins based on second-order information)","Dense-and-Sparse decomposition (storing outliers and sensitive weights in a sparse format)"],"problems":["High memory and resource requirements for inference with large language models (LLMs)","Performance degradation in ultra-low precision quantization of LLMs","Inefficiency of existing quantization methods for single-batch inference due to memory bandwidth bottleneck"],"datasets":["C4","WikiText2","MMLU","Vicuna"],"metrics":["Perplexity","Inference latency/speedup"],"results":[{"metric":"Perplexity (LLaMA-7B, 3-bit, C4)","value":"7.75 (sensitivity-based non-uniform quantization), improved to 7.58 with Dense-and-Sparse decomposition"},{"metric":"Perplexity gap reduction (3-bit quantization vs. FP16 baseline)","value":"up to 2.1x reduction compared to state-of-the-art methods under same memory constraint"},{"metric":"Inference speedup (A6000 GPU)","value":"up to 2.3x (and up to 2.4x in some sections) compared to FP16 baseline"},{"metric":"Perplexity improvement over uniform quantization (LLaMA-7B, 3-bit, C4)","value":"from 28.26 (uniform) to 7.75 (sensitivity-based non-uniform)"}],"novelty_claims":["Introduction of a post-training quantization framework (SqueezeLLM) that enables lossless compression to ultra-low precisions (as low as 3-bit) without performance degradation.","Proposal of sensitivity-based non-uniform quantization, which allocates quantization bins based on second-order information to optimize bit precision assignment.","Dense-and-Sparse decomposition technique that efficiently stores outlier and sensitive weights in a sparse format, improving quantization performance at low bit-widths.","Demonstration of significant reduction in perplexity gap and inference latency compared to state-of-the-art quantization methods under the same memory constraints."]}

methods: SqueezeLLM post-training quantization framework
Sensitivity-based non-uniform quantization (allocating quantization bins based on second-order information)
Dense-and-Sparse decomposition (storing outliers and sensitive weights in a sparse format)
problems: High memory and resource requirements for inference with large language models (LLMs)
Performance degradation in ultra-low precision quantization of LLMs
Inefficiency of existing quantization methods for single-batch inference due to memory bandwidth bottleneck

results.value: 7.75 (sensitivity-based non-uniform quantization), improved to 7.58 with Dense-and-Sparse decomposition

results.metric: Perplexity gap reduction (3-bit quantization vs. FP16 baseline)
results.value: up to 2.1x reduction compared to state-of-the-art methods under same memory constraint
up to 2.3x (and up to 2.4x in some sections) compared to FP16 baseline

results.metric: Perplexity improvement over uniform quantization (LLaMA-7B, 3-bit, C4)

novelty_claims: Introduction of a post-training quantization framework (SqueezeLLM) that enables lossless compression to ultra-low precisions (as low as 3-bit) without performance degradation.
Proposal of sensitivity-based non-uniform quantization, which allocates quantization bins based on second-order information to optimize bit precision assignment.
Dense-and-Sparse decomposition technique that efficiently stores outlier and sensitive weights in a sparse format, improving quantization performance at low bit-widths.
Demonstration of significant reduction in perplexity gap and inference latency compared to state-of-the-art quantization methods under the same memory constraints.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Activation-aware Weight Quantization (AWQ): a hardware-friendly low-bit weight-only quantization method for LLMs that protects salient weights by observing activations and uses per-channel scaling to minimize quantization error without backpropagation or reconstruction.","TinyChat: an efficient inference framework for deploying 4-bit quantized LLMs on edge devices, featuring on-the-fly dequantization, efficient 4-bit weight packing, and kernel fusion."],"problems":["Reducing the memory footprint and accelerating inference of large language models (LLMs) for deployment on edge devices and resource-constrained hardware.","Maintaining high accuracy and generalization ability of LLMs under low-bit quantization, avoiding overfitting to calibration sets and supporting various domains and modalities.","Overcoming the inefficiency and accuracy degradation of existing quantization methods (QAT and PTQ) for LLMs, especially at low bit-widths."],"datasets":["LLaMA (various sizes)","OPT (various sizes)","Vicuna (instruction-tuned LMs)","OpenFlamingo (multi-modal LMs)"],"metrics":["Language modeling accuracy (e.g., perplexity)","Common sense QA accuracy","Domain-specific benchmark scores","Inference speedup (relative to FP16 and GPTQ)","Memory footprint reduction"],"results":[{"metric":"Inference speedup over FP16 (Huggingface)","value":"3.2-3.3x average speedup across LLMs on desktop, laptop, and mobile GPUs"},{"metric":"Inference speedup over GPTQ","value":"1.45x"},{"metric":"Inference speedup over cuBLAS FP16","value":"1.85x"},{"metric":"Memory footprint reduction","value":"~4x lower memory footprint with 4-bit quantization"},{"metric":"Interactive pace for 13B LLM on RTX 4070 (8GB)","value":"30 tokens/second"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a hardware-friendly, activation-aware weight quantization (AWQ) method that protects only a small fraction of salient weights by generalization and hardware efficiency.","AWQ achieves state-of-the-art quantization performance for instruction-tuned and, for the first time, multi-modal LLMs.","AWQ is implemented in TinyChat, enabling practical deployment of 4-bit LLMs on edge devices with significant speedup and memory savings.","AWQ has been widely adopted in industry and open-source frameworks, demonstrating its methods: Activation-aware Weight Quantization (AWQ): a hardware-friendly low-bit weight-only quantization method for LLMs that protects salient weights by observing activations and uses per-channel scaling to minimize quantization error without backpropagation or reconstruction.
TinyChat: an efficient inference framework for deploying 4-bit quantized LLMs on edge devices, featuring on-the-fly dequantization, efficient 4-bit weight packing, and kernel fusion.
problems: Reducing the memory footprint and accelerating inference of large language models (LLMs) for deployment on edge devices and resource-constrained hardware.
Maintaining high accuracy and generalization ability of LLMs under low-bit quantization, avoiding overfitting to calibration sets and supporting various domains and modalities.
Overcoming the inefficiency and accuracy degradation of existing quantization methods (QAT and PTQ) for LLMs, especially at low bit-widths.
metrics: Inference speedup (relative to FP16 and GPTQ)

results.value: 3.2-3.3x average speedup across LLMs on desktop, laptop, and mobile GPUs

results.metric: Interactive pace for 13B LLM on RTX 4070 (8GB)

novelty_claims: Proposes a hardware-friendly, activation-aware weight quantization (AWQ) method that protects only a small fraction of salient weights by observing activations, not weights, and uses per-channel scaling to minimize quantization error.
AWQ does not require backpropagation, reconstruction, or data layout reordering, thus preserving generalization and hardware efficiency.
AWQ achieves state-of-the-art quantization performance for instruction-tuned and, for the first time, multi-modal LLMs.
AWQ is implemented in TinyChat, enabling practical deployment of 4-bit LLMs on edge devices with significant speedup and memory savings.
AWQ has been widely adopted in industry and open-source frameworks, demonstrating its practical impact.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["I-LLM: Integer-only fully-quantized PTQ framework for LLMs","Fully-Smooth Block-Reconstruction (FSBR) for smoothing inter-channel activation and weight variations","Dynamic Integer-only MatMul (DI-MatMul) for dynamic quantization in integer-only matrix multiplication","DI-ClippedSoftmax, DI-Exp, and DI-Normalization: integer-only implementations of non-linear operators using bit shift"],"problems":["Reducing floating-point operations in LLM inference to enable efficient deployment on edge and cloud devices","Addressing large fluctuations in activations across channels and tokens in LLMs that hinder integer-only quantization","Enabling accurate and efficient low-bit (e.g., 4-bit) integer-only quantization for LLMs, including non-linear operators"],"datasets":["LLaMA2-7B","LLAMA-13b"],"metrics":["Accuracy","Perplexity"],"results":[{"metric":"Perplexity reduction (LLAMA-13b)","value":"~20% reduction compared to SOTA non-integer quantization techniques"},{"metric":"Accuracy (W4A4 quantization)","value":"Negligible loss compared to FP baseline"}],"novelty_claims":["First integer-only quantization framework for LLMs, enabling deployment on devices without floating-point units","FSBR method for smoothing all suitable activation-activation and activation-weight pairs, improving quantization accuracy","DI-MatMul for dynamic, integer-only quantization in matrix multiplication, adapting to input range and reducing quantization errors","Integer-only implementations of non-linear operators (DI-ClippedSoftmax, DI-Exp, DI-Norm) using bit shift, maintaining accuracy while replacing complex math","Demonstrates that integer-only quantization can achieve comparable accuracy to floating-point baselines and outperform non-integer quantization methods"]}

methods: I-LLM: Integer-only fully-quantized PTQ framework for LLMs
Fully-Smooth Block-Reconstruction (FSBR) for smoothing inter-channel activation and weight variations
Dynamic Integer-only MatMul (DI-MatMul) for dynamic quantization in integer-only matrix multiplication
DI-ClippedSoftmax, DI-Exp, and DI-Normalization: integer-only implementations of non-linear operators using bit shift
problems: Reducing floating-point operations in LLM inference to enable efficient deployment on edge and cloud devices
Addressing large fluctuations in activations across channels and tokens in LLMs that hinder integer-only quantization
Enabling accurate and efficient low-bit (e.g., 4-bit) integer-only quantization for LLMs, including non-linear operators

results.value: ~20% reduction compared to SOTA non-integer quantization techniques
Negligible loss compared to FP baseline

novelty_claims: First integer-only quantization framework for LLMs, enabling deployment on devices without floating-point units
FSBR method for smoothing all suitable activation-activation and activation-weight pairs, improving quantization accuracy
DI-MatMul for dynamic, integer-only quantization in matrix multiplication, adapting to input range and reducing quantization errors
Integer-only implementations of non-linear operators (DI-ClippedSoftmax, DI-Exp, DI-Norm) using bit shift, maintaining accuracy while replacing complex math
Demonstrates that integer-only quantization can achieve comparable accuracy to floating-point baselines and outperform non-integer quantization methods

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA): freezing pre-trained model weights and injecting trainable rank decomposition matrices into each layer of the Transformer architecture"],"problems":["Reducing the number of trainable parameters required for adapting large pre-trained language models to downstream tasks","Reducing GPU memory requirements and hardware barriers for fine-tuning large language models","Avoiding increased inference latency and sequence length reduction introduced by other parameter-efficient adaptation methods","Maintaining or improving model quality compared to full fine-tuning while increasing efficiency"],"datasets":["RoBERTa","DeBERTa","GPT-2","GPT-3"],"metrics":["Number of trainable parameters","GPU memory requirement","Model quality (task-specific metrics, e.g., accuracy, perplexity, etc.)","Training throughput","Inference latency"],"results":[{"metric":"Reduction in trainable parameters (GPT-3 175B)","value":"10,000x fewer trainable parameters compared to full fine-tuning"},{"metric":"Reduction in GPU memory requirement","value":"3x lower compared to full fine-tuning"},{"metric":"Model quality (RoBERTa, DeBERTa, GPT-2, GPT-3)","value":"On-par or better than full fine-tuning"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes LoRA, a method that injects trainable low-rank matrices into Transformer layers while freezing pre-trained weights, drastically reducing the number of trainable parameters needed for adaptation.","LoRA achieves comparable or better model quality than full fine-tuning with significantly fewer trainable parameters and lower memory requirements.","LoRA introduces no additional inference latency, unlike other parameter-efficient adaptation methods.","LoRA is orthogonal and can be combined with other adaptation methods such as prefix-tuning.","Provides empirical investigation into the rank-deficiency of language model adaptation, supporting the efficacy of LoRA."]}

methods: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA): freezing pre-trained model weights and injecting trainable rank decomposition matrices into each layer of the Transformer architecture
problems: Reducing the number of trainable parameters required for adapting large pre-trained language models to downstream tasks
Reducing GPU memory requirements and hardware barriers for fine-tuning large language models
Avoiding increased inference latency and sequence length reduction introduced by other parameter-efficient adaptation methods
Maintaining or improving model quality compared to full fine-tuning while increasing efficiency
metrics: Model quality (task-specific metrics, e.g., accuracy, perplexity, etc.)

results.value: 10,000x fewer trainable parameters compared to full fine-tuning
On-par or better than full fine-tuning

novelty_claims: Proposes LoRA, a method that injects trainable low-rank matrices into Transformer layers while freezing pre-trained weights, drastically reducing the number of trainable parameters needed for adaptation.
LoRA achieves comparable or better model quality than full fine-tuning with significantly fewer trainable parameters and lower memory requirements.
LoRA introduces no additional inference latency, unlike other parameter-efficient adaptation methods.
LoRA is orthogonal and can be combined with other adaptation methods such as prefix-tuning.
Provides empirical investigation into the rank-deficiency of language model adaptation, supporting the efficacy of LoRA.

Periodic merging of low-rank factors into quantized full-rank weight matrices
Exponential scheduling of projection/merge intervals
Per-layer gradient updates for very large models

novelty_claims: First zero-shot, calibration-free PTQ method for LLMs based on Adaptive LASSO shrinkage method
Does not require any calibration or external data, relying only on model weights
Applies separate quantization scales to outlier and non-outlier weights, eliminating all floating-point weights in the quantized model (unlike other methods)
Provides an information-theoretic foundation for PTQ, showing minimization of KL-divergence and preservation of Shannon information content
Significantly reduces quantization time (at least 10x faster) while maintaining state-of-the-art accuracy
First to study quantization from an information-theoretic perspective using Adaptive LASSO

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Dual-Binarization (DB-LLM)","Flexible Dual Binarization (FDB)","Deviation-Aware Distillation (DAD)"],"problems":["Accuracy degradation in ultra-low-bit (≤2-bit) quantization of large language models (LLMs)","Inefficiency and poor representation capability of binarized weights in LLMs","Distortion and bias in prediction preferences of low-bit LLMs, especially for ambiguous samples","High memory and computation consumption of LLMs impeding deployment on resource-limited devices"],"datasets":["WikiText2","LLaMA-1-30B","LLaMA-1-65B","LLaMA-1-7B","LLaMA-2 family (7B to 70B)"],"metrics":["Perplexity","Computational consumption (e.g., reduction percentage)"],"results":[{"metric":"Perplexity (LLaMA-1-30B, 2-bit)","value":"5.52"},{"metric":"Perplexity (LLaMA-1-65B, 2-bit)","value":"4.84"},{"metric":"Perplexity (LLaMA-1-7B, full-precision)","value":"5.68"},{"metric":"Perplexity (SOTA, 2-bit)","value":"9.64"},{"metric":"Perplexity (DB-LLM, 2-bit)","value":"7.23"},{"metric":"Computational consumption reduction compared to SOTA (same bit-width)","value":"20%"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a novel Dual-Binarization method (DB-LLM) for accurate and efficient 2-bit quantization of LLMs.","Introduces Flexible Dual Binarization (FDB), which splits 2-bit quantized weights into two independent binaries, maximizing representation capability and efficiency.","Presents Deviation-Aware Distillation (DAD) to mitigate prediction distortion in low-bit LLMs by re-weighting distillation loss based on sample ambiguity.","DB-LLM achieves state-of-the-art perplexity and computational efficiency, outperforming previous ultra-low-bit quantization methods."]}

methods: Dual-Binarization (DB-LLM)
Flexible Dual Binarization (FDB)
Deviation-Aware Distillation (DAD)
problems: Accuracy degradation in ultra-low-bit (≤2-bit) quantization of large language models (LLMs)
Inefficiency and poor representation capability of binarized weights in LLMs
Distortion and bias in prediction preferences of low-bit LLMs, especially for ambiguous samples
High memory and computation consumption of LLMs impeding deployment on resource-limited devices

results.metric: Computational consumption reduction compared to SOTA (same bit-width)

novelty_claims: Proposes a novel Dual-Binarization method (DB-LLM) for accurate and efficient 2-bit quantization of LLMs.
Introduces Flexible Dual Binarization (FDB), which splits 2-bit quantized weights into two independent binaries, maximizing representation capability and efficiency.
Presents Deviation-Aware Distillation (DAD) to mitigate prediction distortion in low-bit LLMs by re-weighting distillation loss based on sample ambiguity.
DB-LLM achieves state-of-the-art perplexity and computational efficiency, outperforming previous ultra-low-bit quantization methods.

Language modeling calibration dataset (for model compression)
metrics: Performance relative to 16-bit baseline (accuracy, not specified in detail)

results.value: Minor performance degradations compared to 16-bit baseline
LQ-LoRA outperforms strong QLoRA and GPTQ-LoRA baselines

novelty_claims: Proposes a new matrix decomposition for LLM finetuning: high-precision low-rank plus quantized component (LQ-LoRA)
Introduces an integer linear programming approach for dynamic, per-matrix quantization configuration under a global memory budget
Presents a data-aware decomposition using Fisher information matrix weighting for improved quantization
Enables aggressive quantization (sub-3 bits) with only minor performance loss, outperforming prior QLoRA and GPTQ-LoRA methods
Demonstrates LQ-LoRA as both a finetuning and post-training quantization (PTQ) method for model compression

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["T-MAC: a lookup table (LUT)-based method for efficient low-bit LLM inference on CPUs","Bit-wise table lookup transformation for mixed precision GEMM (mpGEMM)","LUT-centric data layout for on-chip memory residency and parallel lookup","Table quantization and mirror consolidation to reduce LUT size"],"problems":["Efficient deployment of low-bit quantized LLMs on edge devices with CPUs","High inference overhead due to dequantization in existing mpGEMM implementations","Lack of unified and scalable mpGEMM solutions for arbitrary mixed precision (low-bit weights, high-bit activations)","Inefficiency of current hardware and kernels for mixed precision matrix multiplication"],"datasets":["Llama-2-7B-2bit","Llama-2-7B-4bit","Llama-2-13B-4bit","BitNet-b1.58-3B"],"metrics":["Throughput (tokens/s)","Kernel speedup (x)","End-to-end (e2e) inference speedup (x)","Energy consumption reduction (%)"],"results":[{"metric":"Throughput (BitNet-b1.58-3B, 1 core, M2 Ultra)","value":"30 tokens/s"},{"metric":"Throughput (BitNet-b1.58-3B, 8 cores, M2 Ultra)","value":"71 tokens/s"},{"metric":"Throughput (BitNet-b1.58-3B, Raspberry Pi 5)","value":"11 tokens/s"},{"metric":"Kernel speedup (vs llama.cpp, CPU)","value":"up to 6.6x, average 3.6x"},{"metric":"End-to-end LLM inference speedup (Llama-2-7B-2bit)","value":"2.8x"},{"metric":"Energy consumption reduction (vs llama.cpp)","value":"60-70%"}],"novelty_claims":["First practical solution for deploying low-bit LLMs on edge CPUs without relying on GPUs","Transforms data-type-centric multiplication to bit-wise table lookup for unified and scalable mpGEMM","Supports any bit-width combination of activation and weight, enabling flexible mixed precision","Proposes both system and algorithmic techniques for efficient LUT residency and parallel lookup on CPUs","Achieves significant speedup and energy savings for LLM inference on commodity edge CPUs"]}

methods: T-MAC: a lookup table (LUT)-based method for efficient low-bit LLM inference on CPUs
Bit-wise table lookup transformation for mixed precision GEMM (mpGEMM)
LUT-centric data layout for on-chip memory residency and parallel lookup
Table quantization and mirror consolidation to reduce LUT size
problems: Efficient deployment of low-bit quantized LLMs on edge devices with CPUs
High inference overhead due to dequantization in existing mpGEMM implementations
Lack of unified and scalable mpGEMM solutions for arbitrary mixed precision (low-bit weights, high-bit activations)
Inefficiency of current hardware and kernels for mixed precision matrix multiplication
novelty_claims: First practical solution for deploying low-bit LLMs on edge CPUs without relying on GPUs
Transforms data-type-centric multiplication to bit-wise table lookup for unified and scalable mpGEMM
Supports any bit-width combination of activation and weight, enabling flexible mixed precision
Proposes both system and algorithmic techniques for efficient LUT residency and parallel lookup on CPUs
Achieves significant speedup and energy savings for LLM inference on commodity edge CPUs

methods: Lottery Ticket Scratcher (LTS): a heuristic method that freezes weights during quantization-aware training (QAT) once the distance between a full-precision weight and its quantization level is below a threshold, thereby zeroing out their gradients for the remainder of training
problems: Reducing unnecessary weight updates during quantization-aware training (QAT) of neural networks
Lowering computational costs (FLOPs) during QAT without sacrificing model performance
Addressing weight oscillations in low-bit quantized networks

results.metric: FLOPs of backward pass reduced (2-bit MobileNetV2)
FLOPs of backward pass reduced (2-bit ResNet-18)

novelty_claims: First to observe that a large portion of quantized weights reach their optimal quantization level early in QAT, termed as the 'partly scratch-off lottery ticket'.
Proposes the LTS method to freeze such weights, reducing unnecessary gradient calculations and computational cost during QAT.
Demonstrates that LTS can eliminate 50%-70% of weight updating and 25%-35% of backward FLOPs, while maintaining or improving accuracy compared to standard QAT.
Shows that LTS is fundamentally different from sparse training: it focuses on sparse weight gradients, does not prune weights, and is specialized for quantized networks.
